text: This is a pretty straightforward robotic repair of an incarcerated right inguinal hernia.
I did have to reduce the contents preoperatively.
The surgeon works on creating a preperitoneal flap, manipulating the tissue layers to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon encounters adhesions around the infraepigastric vessels, carefully creating space and reorienting the surgical approach to access the desired window for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully pulls the pocket away from the abdominal wall, noting the minimal presence of lateral fat to avoid drifting into the abdominal wall.
The surgeon maneuvers within the supravesicular space, moving instruments back and forth to manipulate the tissue in this area.
Changing my angle of the camera to my right lateral oblique angle. Always helps me to differentiate.
The surgeon uses electrocautery on the sac, carefully pulling surrounding structures and fat to allow them to separate and fall away.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz directly on the sac, targeting fatty tissue to push it laterally and allow it to sweep downwards.
The surgeon approaches the edge of the sac, revealing the vas deferens. A small cord lipoma is pulled out alongside the vas deferens.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens on the right side of the surgical field.
Right there, you can see it.
The surgeon opens up the fat tissue to orient themselves towards the Cooper's ligaments and pubic tubercles, revealing underlying anatomical landmarks.
The edge of the sac is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon searches for a cord lipoma, examining the surrounding tissue for its presence.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the corner, ensuring to maintain distance from the iliac vessels during the dissection.

The surgeon performs bilateral suprapubic fixation, with high medial positioning. The muscles are visible in front of the surgeon, and there are no identifiable vascular structures obstructing the view.
